message.content:
Adversaries may use hidden users to conceal the presence of user accounts they create or modify, making detection and monitoring more challenging. This technique is employed across various operating systems, including macOS, Windows, and Linux. In macOS, adversaries manipulate plist files, folder attributes, and user attributes to hide users, often setting the userID below 500 and modifying the Hide500Users key. On Windows, registry keys are altered to prevent user accounts from appearing on the logon screen. In Linux, the method varies by Display Manager, such as using the gsettings command on GNOME Display Manager to hide users from the login screen.

The potential impact includes unauthorized access and persistence, as hidden users can be used to maintain control over compromised systems without detection. Common exploitation methods involve command-line utilities and registry modifications.

Mitigation strategies include regular audits of user accounts and system configurations, monitoring for unusual changes in user attributes or registry keys, and employing endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions to detect and alert on suspicious activities related to user account management. Additionally, implementing strict access controls and user account policies can help prevent unauthorized account creation and modification.
